summary:
The Potosí Silver Mines of Bolivia (discovered 1545 AD) were the largest silver deposit ever found, producing approximately half of all the silver mined in the world between 1500 and 1800 and fundamentally altering the global economy by flooding Europe and Asia with New World silver, financing the [[event:101|Spanish colonial empire]], and integrating the Americas into a global monetary system for the first time. The Cerro Rico ('Rich Mountain') at Potosí was discovered in 1545 at an altitude of 4,090 meters in what is now Bolivia; within decades the surrounding city had grown to over 160,000 people — one of the largest cities in the world, dependent entirely on silver extraction and its ancillary trades.

The mining operation was powered by the mita forced labor system — a Spanish adaptation of the Inca mit'a — which required indigenous communities within a vast radius to supply workers for six-month rotations in the mines. Conditions were lethal: exposure to mercury (used in the amalgamation process to extract silver from ore), cave-ins, inadequate ventilation, and extreme altitude killed workers in enormous numbers. An estimated 8 million indigenous and enslaved African workers died at Potosí over the colonial period — the figure behind the expression 'the price of silver.' The silver was minted into pesos de ocho (pieces of eight) at Potosí's own mint (the Casa de la Moneda) and transported via the [[event:3628|treasure fleets]] to Spain and via the [[event:2426|Manila Galleon]] to China, where silver's purchasing power was higher than anywhere else due to China's bimetallic monetary system. The influx of American silver caused the European 'Price Revolution' of the 16th–17th centuries — sustained inflation driven by monetary expansion rather than economic growth.
